preamble: The shift from the voiceless velar fricative [x] to the palatal glide [j] (represented here as 'y') is a form of palatalization and fronting, often documented in historical chain shifts where back consonants or labials move toward the palate, particularly before front vowels.

examples.note: Widespread shift of velar fricatives to alveolo-palatals before high front vowels (e.g., in the Pinyin 'x' series where it often precedes the front rounded vowel [y]).
Movement of voiced back consonants toward the palatal glide (e.g., OE geard > yard), which parallels the shift of back fricatives toward palatal positions.

sources: Historical English and Chinese phonology research.
